attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old retired man presents to the psychiatric clinic with an 18 month history of severe major depressive disorder. It is his first experience of this disorder. He has trialled Escitalopram, Desvenlafaxine and Mirtazapine for his symptoms, all of which have provided insufficient relief. He has heard about brain stimulation techniques used in psychiatry and would like your advice.

He has a medical history of right carotid stenosis 10 years ago which was managed with a carotid stent of metallic structure. He takes 75mg Clopidogrel. He also has hypertension, which is well-controlled with 5mg/5mg Perindopril/Amlodipine.

A full workup for organic causes of depression was undergone with no clear cause found.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following treatment options is most appropriate?
attributes.explanation:
Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) and transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) are the two most commonly used brain stimulation techniques in the context of psychiatric illness. In particular, both are appropriate for treatment of major depressive disorder, ECT being more efficacious and TMS has efficacy similar to that of antidepressant medications. However, TMS is contraindicated in this patient, due to his carotid stent. Hence, of these two options, ECT is the only valid one.

Deep brain stimulation is not indicated for use in major depressive disorder, but instead for Parkinson's disease and tremor where medication is not sufficiently effective.

Moclobemide is a monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAOi) which is considered 3rd line in the pharmacological management of major depression. MAOi's are associated with orthostatic hypotension and the risk of hypertensive crisis and thus this option is inappropriate for the patient given their history of hypertension and vascular disease.

Sertraline is an SSRI which is considered first line in pharmacological management of major depression. However given that the patient has failed multiple antidepressants (of which one was an SSRI), this option is inappropriate.

Magnetic seizure therapy (MST) is also a viable consideration when treating major depressive disorder, with similar effectiveness to ECT in inducing generalised tonic-clonic seizures. However, the data pool for MST is limited, with systematic reviews as small as 8 studies showing effectiveness rates that, while promising, are lower than those established by ECT1. Its side effect profile is more favourable than that of ECT, however, so with more data this could become the preferred method of treatment for major depressive disorder in the future1. For this patient, MST is contraindicated due to the patient having a metallic stent. Additionally, MST is not commonly available in Australia for treatment.

Finally, transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) has shown some efficacy in limited trials for the treatment of acute depression. While this treatment may be effective in treating chronic depression, this has not yet been well established by evidence, and so is not an appropriate first line treatment. The evidence surrounding tDCS requires further expansion, replication and comparison with other treatments before it can be a recommended treatment.
learningPoints: Brain stimulation treatment is a currently evolving field of psychiatric treatment for a variety of conditions. However, at this time, ECT is generally the preferred method of treatment for major depressive disorder when brain stimulation treatment is indicated.
ECT is particularly effective for treatment-resistant depression, severe depression with psychotic features, and depression in older adults.
references: Cretaz E, Brunoni AR, Lafer B. Magnetic Seizure Therapy for Unipolar and Bipolar Depression: A Systematic Review. Neural Plast. 2015;2015:521398. doi: 10.1155/2015/521398. Epub 2015 May 13. PMID: 26075100; PMCID: PMC4444586. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26075100/

Baghai TC, Möller HJ. Electroconvulsive therapy and its different indications. Dialogues Clin Neurosci. 2008;10(1):105-17. doi: 10.31887/DCNS.2008.10.1/tcbaghai. PMID: 18472488; PMCID: PMC3181862. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18472488/

Salik I, Marwaha R. Electroconvulsive Therapy. 2022 Sep 19. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan–. PMID: 30855854. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30855854/

Rojas M, Ariza D, Ortega Á, Riaño-Garzón ME, Chávez-Castillo M, Pérez JL, Cudris-Torres L, Bautista MJ, Medina-Ortiz O, Rojas-Quintero J, Bermúdez V. Electroconvulsive Therapy in Psychiatric Disorders: A Narrative Review Exploring Neuroendocrine-Immune Therapeutic Mechanisms and Clinical Implications. Int J Mol Sci. 2022 Jun 22;23(13):6918. doi: 10.3390/ijms23136918. PMID: 35805923; PMCID: PMC9266340. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35805923/
